perturbation.original_text: The obligations of a Recipient under Section 1.1 hereof shall not apply to the following: 2.1. Any Information which, at the time of disclosure, is already rightfully possessed by Recipient or is already in the public domain; 2.2. Any Information which, after the disclosure, enters the public domain without any action or fault of Recipient. 2.3. Any Information which is obtained, other than in connection with the Matter, from any individual, firm or entity which had the unrestricted right to disclose it; 2.4. Any Information which is developed independently by Recipient without reference to any Information or Item;
perturbation.changed_text: The obligations of a Recipient under Section 1.1 hereof shall not apply to the following: 2.1. Any Information which, at the time of disclosure, is already rightfully possessed by Recipient or is already in the public domain; 2.2. Any Information which, after the disclosure, enters the public domain with action or fault of Recipient. 2.3. Any Information which is obtained, other than in connection with the Matter, from any individual, firm or entity which had the unrestricted right to disclose it; 2.4. Any Information which is developed independently by Recipient without reference to any Information or Item;
perturbation.explanation: Modifying section 2.2 to state that the obligations of the Recipient do not apply to information that enters the public domain *with* action or fault of the Recipient directly contradicts the purpose of the Non-Disclosure Agreement, which seeks to protect Disclosing Party's Information. By adding “with action or fault of Recipient” to the reasons that the obligation don't apply means that the Recipient can disclose the confidential information but still don't have any obligation in the disclosure, contradicting the main essence of the contract.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Non-Disclosure. Recipient agrees as follows: 1.1. For a period of three (3) years from the date of entering in to this Agreement, not to disclosure any Information or Item, in any manner whatsoever, directly or indirectly, whether in whole or in part, without the prior written consent of Disclosing Party, to any individual, firm or entity, excepting solely an individual <PAGE> 2 who (a) is an officer, director or employee of Disclosing Party or of the Recipient and (b) has a clear need to know for purposes directly related to the Matter.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies section 2.2 to state that the obligations of the Recipient do not apply to information that enters the public domain *with* action or fault of the Recipient. This directly contradicts section 1.1 of the Non-Disclosure agreement, which states that the Recipient agrees not to disclose any information without prior written consent. The changed text introduces a scenario where the Recipient *can* disclose confidential information (by their own action or fault) without violating the agreement, thereby creating a clear and direct conflict with the non-disclosure obligation outlined in section 1.1.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall require the third party to execute a non-disclosure agreement in form, and substance satisfactory to Disclosing Party. Recipient shall promptly furnish Disclosing Party with a copy of all such non-disclosure agreements.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall require the third party to execute a non-disclosure agreement; however, the Recipient is not responsible for the form or substance of the Non-disclosure agreement. Recipient shall promptly furnish Disclosing Party with a copy of all such non-disclosure agreements.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates that the third-party NDA must be satisfactory to the Disclosing Party. The changed text removes this requirement, stating the Recipient is not responsible for the form or substance of the NDA, contradicting the need for the agreement to protect the Disclosing Party's interests. This makes the purpose of Section 3 unclear - is the Recipient responsible for the Third Party NDA protecting the Disclosing Party, or not?
perturbation.contradicted_text: Non-Disclosure. Recipient agrees as follows: 1.1. For a period of three (3) years from the date of entering in to this Agreement, not to disclosure any Information or Item, in any manner whatsoever, directly or indirectly, whether in whole or in part, without the prior written consent of Disclosing Party, to any individual, firm or entity, excepting solely an individual <PAGE> 2 who (a) is an officer, director or employee of Disclosing Party or of the Recipient and (b) has a clear need to know for purposes directly related to the Matter.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the requirement that the third-party NDA be satisfactory to the Disclosing Party. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which states that the Recipient agrees not to disclose any information without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party. The original text ensured the NDA protected the Disclosing Party, whereas the changed text does not, creating a contradiction as defined by the Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction perturbation type. The change introduces ambiguity regarding the level of protection afforded to the Disclosing Party's information, potentially conflicting with the non-disclosure obligations outlined in the contradicted section.

perturbation.original_text: At the end of our discussions regarding the Matter, the Disclosing Party has the right to request that the Recipient return all Information or Items to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: At the end of our discussions regarding the Matter, the Disclosing Party may request that the Recipient return all Information or Items to the Disclosing Party. However, the Recipient has no obligation to return the Information or Items.
perturbation.explanation: The original text gives the Disclosing Party the *right* to request the return of information, implying an obligation for the Recipient to comply. The modified text explicitly removes this obligation, creating an contradiction between Disclosing party requesting for a return and Recipient's duty to return the Information or Items. This in-text contradiction impacts the purpose of Non-Disclosure and creates uncertainty over what Recipient can do with the information. This contradicts section 8, of preservation of markings of proprietary data.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Preservation of Markings. Recipient shall not alter or delete any markings on any Information or Item restricting its disclosure, use or reproduction or indicating its proprietary or private status. Recipient shall include all such markings in all copies of any Information which it makes.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a contradiction because while the Recipient must still preserve any markings of proprietary data (Contradicted Text), they have no obligation to return the data or items (Changed Text). This creates a conflict because the Recipient can possess information with proprietary markings but is not obligated to return it, undermining the preservation of markings. The changed text directly contradicts the purpose of Non-Disclosure and creates uncertainty over what Recipient can do with the information.

perturbation.original_text: This agreement constitutes the entire, final and complete agreement between the parties relevant to the subject matter hereof, and it supersedes and replaces all written and oral agreements relevant to the subject matter made or existing by and between the parties or their representatives, and there shall be no modifications hereto unless it is in writing signed by the parties.
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement constitutes the agreement between the parties relevant to the subject matter hereof, and it supersedes and replaces all written and oral agreements relevant to the subject matter made or existing by and between the parties or their representatives, and there can be modifications hereto even if is not in writing signed by the parties, as long as both parties agree to the modification in a verbal conversation.
perturbation.explanation: The original text includes an integration clause, indicating that this is the final and complete agreement. The change permits verbal modifications, directly contradicting the integration clause and potentially undermining the certainty and enforceability of the written agreement. This creates an in-text contradiction with all the sections and their enforcement, because it states in the text that any of the sections can be modified, as long as the parties agree verbally.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Injunction. In the event of any breach of this Agreement by either party, or any threatened or attempted breach by either party, the other party to this Agreement shall be entitled to an injunction against the breaching party. Nothing contained herein precludes the other party from pursuing any other remedies available hereunder or at law or equity for such breach, including the recovery of damages. 3
perturbation.justification: The original text's integration clause prohibited modifications unless in writing and signed. The changed text allows verbal modifications, directly conflicting with the contradicted 'Injunction' section and the general enforceability of the written agreement. If the contract terms can be modified verbally, then enforcing any specific clause (like the 'Injunction' clause) becomes problematic, as a party could argue that the clause was verbally modified. The change introduces a clear contradiction within the document, undermining the certainty of the written terms. This adheres to the 'Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction' definition because it defines how the contract can be modified inconsistently, making enforcement difficult.
